phases.label: Pain Reduction & Movement Optimisation
goals: Identify and modify impingement-provoking positions (hip flexion + IR + adduction - FADIR)
Restore pain-free ROM within impingement-free arc
Patient education - Cam vs. Pincer morphology, activity modification strategies
cautions: Avoid FADIR position: hip flexion >90° + internal rotation + adduction (classic impingement provocateur)
Avoid deep squatting and prolonged sitting with hip flexed beyond 90°
No high-impact sport until pain controlled and movement quality restored
Surgical referral (hip arthroscopy) if conservative treatment fails at 3 - 4 months - discuss early
progressionMarkers: Hip ROM improving in pain-free range

phases.label: Strengthening & Movement Retraining
goals: Restore hip abductor, extensor, and ER strength in impingement-free range
Retrain movement patterns to reduce FADIR provocation
Return to low-impact sport and recreational activities
cautions: Squat depth: limit to pain-free range - typically 60 - 80° hip flexion
Cue external rotation bias during all closed-chain exercises
Monitor for groin pain with loading - may indicate labral tear component
progressionMarkers: Movement quality: no excessive IR or anterior pelvic tilt in squatting and stair tasks

phases.label: Return to Sport
goals: Full return to sport including cutting and pivoting
Long-term activity modification and strength maintenance
cautions: If symptoms persist beyond 4 months of conservative treatment - surgical referral warranted
Deep squatting sports (powerlifting, wrestling) may require permanent technique modification
Maintain external rotation bias and core stability as permanent strategy

phases.label: Acute Management
goals: Reduce pain and muscle guarding in anterior hip / groin
Maintain hip ROM without aggravating torn fibres
Early pain-free muscle activation to reduce atrophy
Patient education - mechanism, typical 2 - 6 week recovery for Grade I - II
cautions: Avoid passive hip extension stretching in acute phase - risks extending tear
Avoid resisted hip flexion above 90° (provocative position for iliopsoas)
Crutches if antalgic gait - normalise as soon as pain permits
Rule out iliopsoas avulsion (ASIS or lesser trochanter fracture) in adolescents and powerful athletes

phases.label: Progressive Strengthening
goals: Progressive iliopsoas and hip flexor strengthening
Restore full hip ROM including extension
Return to jogging and linear running
cautions: 24h pain rule - pain allowed during exercise must resolve within 24h
Introduce hip extension stretching cautiously from week 3 - 4
No sprinting or kicking until strength ≥80% symmetry

phases.label: Return to Sport
goals: Full sprint speed and kicking power
Return to full training and competition
cautions: Sprint and kicking progressions must be graduated - 50% → 75% → 100% effort
Recurrence prevention: maintain hip flexor strengthening 2 x per week in-season
progressionMarkers: Full sprint at 100% effort without pain or apprehension

phases.label: Acute Phase & Isometric Loading

cautions: Avoid aggressive passive stretching into hip abduction in reactive phase
Rule out pubic stress fracture, avulsion fracture (ASIS/pubic ramus) in young athletes

phases.label: Progressive Strengthening

Avoid extreme hip abduction (side lunges, wide squats) until strength ≥80% symmetry
No pivoting, cutting or kicking until criteria met

phases.label: Return to Sport

phases.label: Pain Management & Education
goals: Reduce pain and improve quality of life
Comprehensive patient education - OA is manageable, not 'bone on bone inevitable surgery'
Identify and modify pain-provoking activities while maintaining movement
Establish walking and gentle exercise as primary long-term intervention
cautions: Avoid high-impact loading (running, jumping) during acute pain flares
Manage patient expectations - OA cannot be 'cured' conservatively but can be well-controlled
Orthopaedic referral if severe pain, radiographic Kellgren-Lawrence Grade III - IV, or loss of function refractory to conservative treatment
NSAIDs, topical diclofenac - appropriate adjunct; not a substitute for exercise
progressionMarkers: Walking 20 min without significant pain increase

phases.label: Progressive Strengthening
goals: Hip and quadriceps strength improvement - key for pain reduction and function in OA
Improve walking speed, stair function, and community mobility
Reduce pain and improve WOMAC and HOOS scores
cautions: Pain should not exceed 5/10 during exercise (tolerable exertion level)
Progress load gradually - OA cartilage has limited tolerance for rapid load increases
progressionMarkers: Stair use with reciprocal pattern and controlled descent

phases.label: Long-term Maintenance
goals: Sustain strength and function gains achieved in Phase 2
Delay progression to THA through active lifestyle
cautions: Exercise is safe and beneficial for hip OA - do not stop exercising during mild flares, modify intensity instead
Annual review - monitor radiographic and functional progression

phases.label: Pain Reduction & Load Modification
goals: Reduce pain associated with snapping (internal: iliopsoas; external: IT band; intra-articular: labral tear)

cautions: Intra-articular snapping (with locking or giving way) - urgent orthopaedic review for labral tear
Avoid provocative hip movements (external: hip flexion-IR arc; internal: leg swing in walking)

EXTERNAL TYPE: IT band tissue loading + gluteus medius strengthening to reduce lateral snap

cautions: Snap may persist during rehab - target is pain-free snap, then eventual reduction in snap frequency

phases.label: Return to Activity

Intra-articular causes - arthroscopic debridement or labral repair indicated if conservative management fails

phases.label: Isometric Loading & Load Management

phases.label: Isometric Loading & Load Management
